content_en:
Ibn Hajar al-Haytami's prayer chapters in Tuhfat al-Muhtaj engage the full depth of accumulated Shafi'i prayer law, addressing not only the standard rulings but the practical questions that had arisen in Muslim communities across the diverse contexts where Shafi'i fiqh was practiced — from Egypt and the Hijaz to Southeast Asia and East Africa.

The chapter on the Basmalah in prayer reflects one of the most widely discussed inter-school differences. The Shafi'i school requires reciting 'Bismillah ar-Rahman ar-Rahim' before al-Fatihah as the first verse of the surah, both because it is the first verse of al-Fatihah (the Shafi'i position) and because omitting it would leave the prayer without a complete al-Fatihah. Ibn Hajar al-Haytami argues this position with reference to the mushaf (Quranic codex) tradition, in which the Basmalah is written at the head of al-Fatihah, and the hadith of Nu'aym al-Mujmir who described hearing the Prophet recite it aloud in prayer.

The question of whether reciting al-Fatihah aloud or silently is a condition of prayer validity generates significant analysis. Ibn Hajar al-Haytami presents the standard Shafi'i positions: al-Fatihah is recited aloud in the first two rak'ahs of Fajr, Maghrib, and 'Isha; silently in all other rak'ahs and prayers. The reason for the loud recitation in these specific prayers is not explained by any Quranic text but by the prophetic practice transmitted through multiple channels.

For the traveler's prayer, Ibn Hajar al-Haytami presents the Shafi'i distance threshold for shortening and combining prayers: 48 miles (approximately 81 kilometers) by the Shafi'i standard, based on the interpretation of 'travel' (safar) in the relevant hadiths. He addresses the common question of a person who travels back and forth frequently — whether their status as 'traveler' is renewed each time or expires after a certain residence period. The standard Shafi'i position is that residence of four or more days in a location with the intention to remain that long terminates the travel status.

Ibn Hajar al-Haytami discusses the imam-follower relationship with particular attention to the question of following an imam of a different legal school. He affirms the standard Shafi'i position that prayer behind an imam of another school is valid, provided the imam's prayer is valid according to his own school. But he adds the important caveat: if the Shafi'i follower knows that the imam has done something that would invalidate the prayer in the Shafi'i school (even if not in the imam's own school), the follower should not pray behind that imam — because validity from the follower's perspective depends on the follower's own legal school's criteria.

The chapter on the funeral prayer is notable for its comprehensive coverage of all aspects of handling the deceased Muslim: washing (ghusl al-mayyit), shrouding (takfin), the funeral prayer itself, and burial. Ibn Hajar al-Haytami presents each step with its conditions and recommended acts, emphasizing that these acts are fard kifayah (communally obligatory) that become the responsibility of the Muslim community upon any Muslim's death.
